name: Château Faugères
winery: Château Faugères sits on the limestone plateau east of Saint-Émilion, with vineyards rolling down toward the Dordogne valley. Acquired by Swiss entrepreneur Silvio Denz in 2005, the estate became the canvas for one of the most striking modern winemaking buildings in Bordeaux — a 'cathedral of wine' built entirely around gravity-fed vinification.
architect_blurb: Mario Botta is the Swiss architect known for the SFMOMA, the Tinguely Museum in Basel, and the Mogno chapel — a body of work organised around geometry, raw masonry, and the play of natural light. He has spoken of architecture as 'a way of articulating the sacred in everyday life,' and Faugères is his only winery in France.
why: Botta turned the winery into a long, dignified prow of pale Dordogne limestone, anchored to the slope by a tall central tower that doubles as the gravity chute for the grapes. Harvest is poured in at the top and travels downward through sorting, fermentation, and barrel aging without pumps — the building is, quite literally, a machine for making wine by gravity alone, dressed as a piece of monastic architecture.

name: Château La Dominique
winery: Owned by the Fayat family since 1969, Château La Dominique sits directly on the boundary with Pomerol, neighbour to Cheval Blanc. The estate's new cellar, completed in 2013, was conceived to be visible from the Saint-Émilion plateau and to give the public a rooftop terrace overlooking the most prestigious vineyards in the appellation.
architect_blurb: Jean Nouvel — Pritzker laureate, author of the Institut du Monde Arabe, the Louvre Abu Dhabi, and the Philharmonie de Paris — has made a career of buildings that engage their context through colour, reflection, and atmosphere rather than through historical pastiche.
why: Nouvel wrapped the cellar in mirrored stainless-steel panels lacquered in five shades of red, so the building reflects vines, sky and the changing light back at the visitor in the colours of the wine itself. The rooftop is filled with thousands of red glass pebbles standing in for a fermenting vat — a public belvedere that turns the working winery into a piece of land art.

name: Château Cheval Blanc
appellation: Saint-Émilion Premier Grand Cru Classé A
winery: Cheval Blanc is one of the most storied estates in Bordeaux, owned by Bernard Arnault and Albéric Bichot's longtime partner Baron Frère. The 1947 vintage is legendary; the 2011 cellar was commissioned to give that level of wine a vat room worthy of it.
architect_blurb: Christian de Portzamparc was the first French architect to win the Pritzker Prize (1994). His work — from the Cité de la Musique in Paris to LVMH's New York tower — is characterised by sculptural, undulating forms and a refusal of right angles where the site allows softer geometry.
why: Rather than build vertically, Portzamparc laid a long white concrete wave across the vineyard. The roof is a green meadow you can walk on; underneath, 52 bespoke concrete vats are arranged so each plot of vines has its own vessel. The building disappears into the landscape from one side and unfurls as a luminous, ship-like canopy from the other — modern architecture that defers to the vines instead of dominating them.

name: Château Le Dôme
winery: Le Dôme is the flagship cuvée of Jonathan Maltus, an English winemaker who has spent three decades buying small, exceptional parcels around Saint-Émilion. The new winery, finished in 2021, was designed to vinify each parcel separately under a single circular roof.
architect_blurb: Norman Foster is the British architect behind the Reichstag dome, Apple Park, and the Hong Kong & Shanghai Bank — a practice built on engineered elegance and the honest expression of structure.
why: Foster's response is a perfect circular building crowned by a radial timber roof of laminated larch, splaying outward like the spokes of a barrel head. The 360° glazed elevation lets visitors see the vineyards from any point in the vat room, and the slender steel columns disappear so the eye reads only wood, vines, and sky. It is one of Foster's smallest projects — and one of his most quietly perfect.

name: Château Les Carmes Haut-Brion
winery: An urban estate inside the city of Bordeaux, Les Carmes Haut-Brion takes its name from the Carmelite friars who tended the vines from the 16th century. Patrick Pichet acquired the property in 2010 and commissioned a new winery that respects the historic walled park and the stream that runs through it.
architect_blurb: Philippe Starck is France's best-known designer, the author of the Juicy Salif lemon squeezer and the interiors of dozens of hotels and apartments worldwide. Luc Arsène-Henry is the Bordeaux-based architect who has executed many of Starck's built projects.
why: Starck took the brief literally: he designed a long, dark, hull-shaped winery that appears to float on the estate's pond like a ship pulled up next to the 18th-century house. Cor-Ten steel forms the prow; reflections double the building in the water. Inside, the vat room is half-buried so the vines remain the tallest things on the property — radical contemporary form, almost invisible from the street.

name: Château Pédesclaux
winery: Founded in 1810 and elevated to fifth growth in the 1855 classification, Pédesclaux had drifted before being bought by the Lorenzetti family in 2009. They committed to a complete rebuild of the technical estate around — but not inside — the existing 19th-century château.
architect_blurb: Jean-Michel Wilmotte is one of France's most prolific museum and heritage architects, responsible for the interiors of the Musée d'Orsay and the renovation of the Collège de France. His practice is defined by sober, glass-and-stone interventions that let older buildings keep their authority.
why: Wilmotte wrapped the original château in a precise glass cube and slid a new gravity-fed cellar into the slope behind it, so the old building floats inside a contemporary case without being touched. The result keeps every stone of the Médoc tradition visible while giving the estate a genuinely 21st-century working winery — heritage and modernity arranged as two clearly separate, mutually respectful objects.

name: Château Lafite Rothschild
winery: Lafite needs no introduction — first growth of the 1855 classification, in the Rothschild family since 1868. By the 1980s the estate had outgrown its existing chai and Baron Éric de Rothschild went looking for an architect who could build a barrel hall worthy of the wine.
architect_blurb: Ricardo Bofill, the Catalan architect of La Muralla Roja and Les Espaces d'Abraxas outside Paris, spent his career reworking classical forms — colonnades, vaults, basilicas — at unfamiliar scales. He died in 2022.
why: Bofill placed the new barrel cellar entirely underground, then carved it as a perfect circle 18 metres in radius, with sixteen slender concrete columns radiating from a central oculus like a buried Roman basilica. The geometry is liturgical; the natural cool of the earth does the climate control. Decades on, it is still the most quietly imitated piece of winery architecture in the world.

name: Château Margaux
winery: Château Margaux's main building is the neoclassical 'Versailles of the Médoc' completed in 1815. Two hundred years later, Corinne Mentzelopoulos commissioned a new technical winery to celebrate the bicentenary — designed to disappear behind the original.
architect_blurb: Norman Foster's brief was unusually restrained: do not compete with the existing palace. The practice has form here — its Reichstag dome and Carré d'Art at Nîmes are textbook examples of modern intervention in historic settings.
why: Foster created a long, light pavilion with slender white columns and a delicate canopy roof that echoes the colonnade of the 1815 château without copying it. A new vinothèque, research laboratory and tasting room sit underneath, and the older buildings around the courtyard are restored rather than replaced. From the famous tree-lined drive the silhouette of the estate is unchanged — the modernity is felt only once you are inside.

name: Château Smith Haut Lafitte & Les Sources de Caudalie
winery: Bought by Daniel and Florence Cathiard in 1990, Smith Haut Lafitte was the project that turned a sleepy Pessac-Léognan estate into the most-visited address in Bordeaux. Adjacent to the winery, the Cathiards built Les Sources de Caudalie — the world's first vinothérapie spa and hotel — opened in 1999.
architect_blurb: The complex was designed by local architects Yves Collet and Patrice Maffre, who specialised in vernacular Médoc and Aquitaine timber construction. Their reference points were tobacco-drying barns, oyster cabins on the Bassin d'Arcachon, and 19th-century glasshouses.
why: Rather than reach for a star architect, the Cathiards assembled a small village of reclaimed timber pavilions raised on stilts above the estate's natural springs. Each building borrows a vernacular type from south-west France and rearranges it for a new purpose — a hotel room that looks like an oyster cabin, a spa under a glasshouse roof. The lesson is that 'modern winery architecture' need not mean a single icon: it can also mean a thoughtful reuse of regional forms.

name: Château Cantenac Brown
winery: The original Cantenac Brown is a Tudor-style folly built in the 1850s by the Scottish painter John-Lewis Brown. Bought by Tristan Le Lous in 2019, the estate has just opened one of the first fully bioclimatic grand cru cellars in Bordeaux.
architect_blurb: Philippe Madec is a French architect and a founding voice of the 'frugal architecture' manifesto, arguing that contemporary buildings should be made from local, low-carbon materials — earth, stone, straw, timber — and should rely on passive climate control wherever possible.
why: Madec built the new winery from raw rammed earth taken directly from the estate, with a structure of regional Landes pine and a thatch of local rye straw. Walls a metre thick keep the vat room at cellar temperature without air conditioning; the carbon footprint is a fraction of a typical Médoc cellar. It is a deliberately quiet, almost archaic-looking building that points to where the next decade of winery architecture is going.

name: Château La Coste (Bordeaux annex tasting room)
winery: Although Château La Coste itself is in Provence, its founder Paddy McKillen has long maintained a Bordeaux office and tasting space dedicated to the great Bordeaux estates he imports. The space references the architecture programme he commissioned in Provence, which set the modern template followed by many Bordeaux estates since.
architect_blurb: Tadao Ando is the self-taught Japanese architect and Pritzker laureate whose vocabulary of board-formed concrete, water, and a single shaft of daylight has shaped contemporary museum and winery design worldwide.
why: Ando's contribution at La Coste — a low concrete art-and-wine pavilion floating in a reflecting pool — proved that a winery building could be a destination as well as a tool, and that minimalist concrete and water could feel as warm as a stone chai. Every Bordeaux estate that has built a contemporary tasting room in the last fifteen years owes something to that template.

name: Château Cos d'Estournel
architect: Louis-Gaspard Estournel (founder); contemporary refit by Jean-Michel Wilmotte
winery: The original Cos was built in the 1820s by Louis-Gaspard Estournel, a Médoc producer who traded heavily with India and returned home obsessed with the architecture he had seen there. The result is the most theatrical château in Bordeaux: golden pagodas, carved teak doors brought back from Zanzibar, and not a single vine in sight from the road.
architect_blurb: Jean-Michel Wilmotte was called back in by owner Michel Reybier in the mid-2000s to give the 19th-century pagoda an entirely new working winery — without disturbing a single tower visible from the route des châteaux.
why: Wilmotte buried the new gravity-fed cellar deep into the slope behind the historic building, so the famous oriental silhouette remains untouched while a glass-and-steel vat room operates invisibly beneath it. Cos shows that 'modern winery architecture' in Bordeaux is sometimes most successful when it refuses to show itself at all — and lets a piece of 19th-century romanticism keep top billing.

name: La Cité du Vin (Bordeaux)
winery: Not a winery but the cultural anchor of wine architecture in Bordeaux: a 14,000 m² museum on the banks of the Garonne dedicated to wine cultures across the world. It opened in 2016 and is the building most international visitors associate with Bordeaux's contemporary identity.
architect_blurb: XTU Architects is a Paris practice founded by Anouk Legendre and Nicolas Desmazières, known for working with double-curved geometry and digital fabrication. La Cité du Vin is their best-known building.
why: The form is famously hard to describe — a swirling, golden-anodised aluminium tower that reads, depending on where you stand, as a decanter, a vine knot, or a wisp of fermenting wine. Built from 900 unique laminated-glass panels and 2,500 individually shaped aluminium scales, it could not have been designed before parametric software. For the first time, Bordeaux has a piece of architecture that signals 'wine city' as immediately as the Opera House signals Sydney.

name: Château Pichon Longueville Comtesse de Lalande
winery: Across the road from its brother estate Pichon Baron, Pichon Comtesse has been owned by the Rouzaud family — proprietors of Champagne Louis Roederer — since 2007. The new winery, completed in 2013, was their first major intervention on the estate.
architect_blurb: Jean-Paul Viguier is the French architect responsible for France Télévisions' headquarters and the Sofitel Chicago Water Tower. His Bordeaux work is typically a calm, horizontal modernism rooted in landscape rather than gesture.
why: Viguier's vat hall sits like a long, low glass band along the gravel ridge — a deliberately quiet building that lets the existing 18th-century château remain the visual centre of the estate. Inside, the room is engineered for plot-by-plot vinification with 36 small stainless tanks. It is what most working second-growth Bordeaux estates actually need from modern architecture: precision, light, and self-effacement.
